items.q: Should a comparative answer analyse one poem fully before the other?
items.correct: No - the strongest answers compare both poems together, point by point.
items.misconception: Yes, finish one poem then start the next.
items.random_wrong: You only need to write about one poem.
items.why: Integrated comparison keeps the texts in dialogue; separate halves read as two mini-essays.

items.q: What makes a good basis for comparison?
items.correct: A shared theme, technique or form that genuinely links the two poems.
items.misconception: Any two poems can be compared on length alone.
items.random_wrong: They must be written by the same poet.
items.why: A clear point of comparison gives focus; surface features like length are not meaningful links.

items.q: What is the role of context (AO3) in a comparison?
items.correct: It helps explain why poems treat a shared subject in different ways.
items.misconception: It means retelling each poet's whole life story.
items.random_wrong: Context is irrelevant to analysing poetry.
items.why: Context should illuminate the poems' differences, not become biography for its own sake.

items.q: A strong point of comparison between two poems is best based on:
items.correct: A shared theme, form or technique
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A shared theme, form or technique. “Their page length” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The word 'whereas' is most useful for signalling:
items.why: Correct answer: A contrast between the poems. “Agreement between poems” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: Discusses both poems together, point by point
items.misconception: Analyses one poem fully, then the other
items.why: Correct answer: Discusses both poems together, point by point. “Analyses one poem fully, then the other” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In comparative analysis, context (AO3) helps explain:
items.correct: Why paired poems treat a subject differently
items.why: It's Why paired poems treat a subject differently. “The cost of the book” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
